author: @Rose.Buchanan.Fine.Artist
text: High quality paint is expensive (Golden being my favorite) and my grandparents got married during the depression…. I don’t waste anything and always wipe the extra paint off my brush onto a paper or canvas- I call those my serendipity paintings. Sometimes they’re important starts for abstract work, and sometimes they just start filling in the canvas weave and I paint over them.

author: @Tempy-c5t
text: I thought it was interesting that you seemed to move more towards your 'comfort' zone in the second layer.  The round shapes started to creep in as you appeared to relax into the piece.  Do you think we are 'hard wired' in some way to return to the shapes we feel the most comfort with?  Where is it that this sense of comfort comes from?  Is it from a time in our younger lives that we felt exceptionally 'relaxed' and 'happy' in?  I really enjoyed watching the tranformation and am looking forward to see how/if you finish it and what your overall reaction to the process and piece is.  Thanking you for sharing your work.  Cathe

text: This was an excellant exercise.  I was totally relaxed and in the moment.  I was drawn to the squares in blue and the triangles in green. I live in QLD, Australia and have been feeling edgy as we're in the path of a cyclone.  it's meant to hit sometime tomorrow or the next day.  Brisbane hasn't had a cyclone for about 50 years and although I want it to be over I don't really want it to begin.  There's ususally so much destruction in the aftermath and really vunerable  people like the homeless and those living on or below the poverty line always seem to suffer the most.

author: @veronicafortune5851
text: I have to tell you that I just watched this video, and I too had a divorce when my son he was about seven and then his sister was about 11 and then his other sister was about 15 when I divorced who is my first and original husband way back when. So I have a lot of feelings about that and I’m gonna try to paint my feelings about that too. I did one painting about something that happened to me as a child and it was my first abstract ever. The counselor has had to paint your feelings, and I did a series of three actually. It was called the furtive memory series. There’s something I cannot remember, and my brother has the same thing. Neither one of us can remember, but we both know that it was something severe enough for us to forget as children or to not remember. And I did a series of on that they were. It looked like three different people did three different paintings, but it was all me. The first one was called the ancestor. The second one was called the actuality and the third one was called the aftermath. I still have all three paintings I won’t part with those. But then about the divorce, I never painted it about that. This will be interesting!!!

author: @Tempy-c5t
text: Thank you for the videos in this series.  You gave me much to think about.  Layers as history!  I didn't realise that you could use the clear gesso to hold the layers in place.  I get frustrated that acrylic paint dries so quickly but, ironically, I also get annoyed that I can't finish a piece in one go.  So I have a few unfinished pieces that I never go back to which also frustrates me.  I used to feel so in touch with my intuition but it feels like it's almost disappeared.  I know it's related to mood-stabaliser medications I need to take to have a somewhat functioning existence.  I only share this as you shared your experience about your divorce and the many challenges you had to face.  I found the finished painting extremely interesting.  A darken figure turned away from what looks a wall facing female and a blank faced child.  Thank you for your courage in telling a part of your story.  The end result of your piece was both beautiful but, full of depth.  Also, thank you for showing what paint and brushes you use.  I am unable to leave the unit I sharee with my husband (no kids/grand-kids) very often and it's so overwhelming trying to shop online for art suppiles.  Some deliveries fill me with joy but dissapointment is also frequent at what I get.  It's also difficult living in Australia.  The population is not high enough to be able to support purchasing the type of paint I like to use in large quantities.  I must stop now. this comment has become to personal and I'll wipe it  and turn it into a surface level/banal comment if I keep going.  Sorry for the emotional dump.  The sadness and dissapointment of where my life is at has decided to be unleashed.

author: @JoyFaheyArtist
text: Hi Cath it's good to let it all out sometimes and all those emotions can be expressed in your paintings which makes them very powerful! I know the feeling of not having access to paint I want, living in Spain our choice is quite limited too! But heyho it' makes us more creative in mixing colors!  I try to turn things around to what I do have rather than what I don't and most importantly we have painting! Aren't we lucky to have that! So keep painting and get lost in creativity where troubles disappear for a while. It's the best healer for everything 😊🎨🖌️💕

author: @LearnHealGrowShine
text:
Before you came back to this painting in the previous video, it seemed the child was between the other figures, one towering over the child and the other coming down to the child's level as though to check in or console, protect or to explain.

Where it sits now, I see the shapes on the outer edges as perhaps two structures, the first figure is facing away from the others, looking towards his new home, life or future. The colours dominant in the mother and child are present in that future.

The mother and child are together, but not necessarily connected. The mother faces away from the viewer (or the artist,) the child faces towards the audience. At the core of their structure (home or future) are the colours of all figures, though the edges echo the background. 


I hesitate to say so, but throughout, I couldn't decide whether the woman had black hair, or a bag over her head. Either way, She's facing/looking at or towards nothing.

As you were painting, before you spoke of your situation, I assumed the child was you, rather than the woman. Though our lives often echo the past, as well, in one form or another.


I'm grateful that art is working through you to heal as you create. What a beautiful journey.

My life has gone down similar paths. Thank you for sharing your work.

author: @helenwinter2115
text: Thank you for sharing Joy. Like you I am now able to stop myself from sliding into & spending time in a negative place. For me I switch to gratitude and how many things in my life I am grateful for. I live in Australia and am so grateful for the freedom we are able to enjoy; the plentiful supply of food available to us and the peace. With so much turmoil in the world, peace is so precious. I am looking forward to this New Year to work on some of your methods of painting and allowing myself to have fun making art. I have worked for many years using watercolors and have only recently changed over to acrylics which for me are so versatile and I love the differences they can produce. I created some really good paintings in watercolor but I feel a greater sense of freedom working with acrylics. Please keep making your fabulous videos, they are really enjoyable to watch and learn from. I feel I have finally found a true art soul mate; I really relate to the way you think about art.  I wish you peace and joy in the coming year.

author: @JoyFaheyArtist

text: Thank you Joy, I am struggling and you have helped. My husband died in April,  he had to have a major surgery which went badly, after the surgery he was in a coma for 19 days and then he died. He was and is the love of my life. we were teenagers when we met, I am 63 now. I am an illustrator but I have felt completely broken and unable to feel any interest in anything let alone drawing, however part of me knows that art can help me even though I don't know how. You seem so genuine and your warmth and honesty have given me a little bit of hope.

author: @JoyFaheyArtist
text: Hello Karen I'm so sorry to hear about your husband, what a difficult time for you especially over Christmas. My painting journey started when my son joined the angels. It took some time but I slowly started healing through doing watercolors of nature. I found solice in the soft colours and studying the trees and shapes all around me. Slowly but surely I found a new life in art and painting and writing a journal. I used to set myself little goals of doing 3 things a day, however small but completing them was a good feeling. I now just think happy thoughts of lovely memories and thank him for my painting life. You'll get there I promise one day at a time. Paint for him and he'll be smiling down on your creativity and courage 😊 Much love 💕🖌️🎨

author: @Thestarsarefire
text: ⁠@JoyFaheyArtist thank you so much for your thoughtful reply. lm so very sorry that you lost your son, the pain you have been through is unimaginable. You inspire me not to give up and l will try the things that helped you and maybe they will help me too. Thank you again x

author: @janetjoslin6463
text:
Your channel popped up on my YouTube feed yesterday and the first video that I watched. Was you talking about not having family at Christmas and spending it on your own. And that is exactly what I was doing because both of my adult children have refused to communicate with me. My daughter has been doing this now for 20 years and turned the rest of the family against me. My daughter is almost 60 years old and my son is in prison and he is 58. I have six grandchildren and a new great grandchild my first, and I have not been allowed to to communicate or see the first grandchild the others are adults and don’t have anything to do with me. I finally given up, trying to communicate, thinking that somehow I could change their minds by expressing my love to them to know avail. I’v gone through many emotions like not understanding how this could happen to me. I know I’m very different as a creative, emotional sensitive authentic and a kind person, but I can’t imagine ever hurting anyone as my own children have done to me. I felt like a victim and sorry for myself and wanted to end it all for years. But finally the last year I have learned to embrace my solitude and actually feel lucky to have the opportunity to just be with myself spiritually having a lot of soul searching and growth! Through everything my art has kept me grounded, knowing who I am. I am an artist, through and through.
I am also born in November 1942 and I am a Scorpio. My artwork has always been about creating beauty your nature, which is always been my love. But after listening to you and seeing your work, I see how I can express my feelings through intuitive art making. I have constantly judged when I start painting as to if it’s going to be good and beautiful like I’ve always done. Actually, before I even watched you I was thinking of doing figures in a very abstract way. And then as life does, I was shown a way to do it through you. I love your gentle nature and kindness, and understanding of what it is like to be a creative and sensitive human being. So I’m gonna take your free course and get started on the process. I paint in my living room and I live in a small mobile home and I wish I could work large and messy. But I’m going to start just using my ketch books and not use anything yet to be an actual painting. I have to trick myself into being the child again and doing whatever feels like me. To tap into that joyful being that doesn’t matter what anybody thinks, especially myself. I want to thank you for touching my heart and I will be watching you!

author: @JoyFaheyArtist
text: Hello Jane thanks so much for sharing, I really understand where you're coming from, indeed I have a very on and off relationship with my son but have learnt to let it be how it is., however heart breaking it can be if I don't catch it in time! It's wonderful to hear you find painting helpful, it's such a healer isn't it? I hope you enjoy the course, thanks so much! Getting it all out on paper is so freeing! I've got some great things in store for the year ahead so I'll keep you posted! Happy painting and Happy New Year! Much love 💕🎨🖌️😊

author: @jayneyharmony
text: Oh Joy. Christmas holiday season really is an emotional time. You’re absolutely right. My two sons have grown up and left. I’m single  in the 50s and I have felt very keenly exactly What you’re describing. I kind of have lost my Mojo with my Art practice after over 10 years of doing it.  Just start something is such great advice. I intend to make 2025 a year of connection because I have a keen. I have such a deep feeling that my relationships and friendships mean more to me than ever before. Which I know is all part of getting older and learning how to exist on my own again. I just want to thank you from the bottom of my heart. You are such a creative lovely positive force out there in the world and I one really appreciate you happy New Year to you and here’s to creativity and how healing and cathartic it can be. I’ll definitely look up your Art intuitive course. It sounds fantastic. Just what I need  🤩And thank you again Joy.  Joy by name and joy by nature xox ❤✍️🙌🖌️🎨 🎉

Today My heart is uplifted and I feel the energy to continue ❤❤❤
